text: Aya-ya, my friend,
He said, "My friend, my friend, my friend, my friend–"
A Spotted-one died in Bugabo–
He brought iron from Itahwa."
Hear the bickering of women
That quickly overturned their reign.
They sigh covetously at what they cannot carry
On Nyabeteka mountain of Luhanga.
May you lack the space to seat your guests.
The packet by a lover wrapped–
Its coffee berries you eat;
Its wrapper you sit and gaze upon."
While I was there . . .
My wife was carrying water to the house–
I had already retired.
When she came to speak to me, that wife of mine–
After she brought the water–
She said, "My husband,
You never go to trade.
You're worth less than the beer jug at your father's place,Half-Full.
You're worth less than the dove, the Trilling-voice.
I'd gone for water when
I saw the traders–real men."
She said, "They'd carried so many things they were throwing some away."
He said, "My wife, if they'd carried so many they were throwing some away,
Why not pick up a few and bring them here
So I might see them too?"
She said, "My husband,
There's more to it than that."
When the man awoke, he cut bananas for beer.
He said, "Ferment some mash for me, if you please. I'm going to trade.
I'll go to Buha of Nyalwongo,
Where a woman grinds grain with her husband on her back.
The days I spend there will be eight.
On the ninth I'd like to find you've brewed the beer for me."
He tied a bundle full of clattering things.
The road for Ilogelo he knew well.
At the house of Bilaze of George
He went to Kamitenga.
He took it straight.
He went to Bigugo, to Kashalala.
He went to Lutenge.
He descended and went to Kyamalebe and Low-water crossing.
He took the road for Lugongo.
When he reached Nyakisingilo,
At Lyambo's and Lukaza-magulu's in Ihangilo,
At Ngalama he went into the town of Kishanda.
He travelled west.
He asked the way to Kibale and Kiwela.
He went to Mikale.
He went to Kabayondo.
Nshamba of Igabilo.
He climbed to Kabale.
In Ihangilo the Kabales are many.
There is Kabale of Mwamali,
Fearless one of Migongo.
It is not a belt tied about your waist.
You have seen it, Pained-one.
Listen, Arouser-of-desire.
From Kitebelo at the Indians'
He took the road to Kahu.
My friend
He went towards Kyeshongele.
He was on the road
My kings!
Ya-ya-ya-ya
He took it towards Itongo of Kajalaba.
You of Luhanga and Kyolele,
Bilabo of Ntagonga.
He took the road.
The pass, he took to Ngote.
He went to Mugolo.
The hill, he descended to Kilibino.
He went to Kamishango.
That's him in Mumpanda.
When he had arrived
Among the thickets of Mubunda of Kitoko...
Ahi Ya ya ya ya-ah
Eye ye ye-eh
Ye ye ye-eh ye-e-eh
Ye-e-eh
It travels like a creeper vine.
May you lack the space to seat your guests.
The packet by a lover wrapped–
Its coffee berries you eat;
Its wrapper you sit and gaze upon, Lujoge."
Then the sun set.
Among the thickets of Mubunda
I met an elder woman digging yams.
I say, "Mother, give me lodging. I need a place to lie down."
"My house cannot be lain in."
"What forbids it to be lain in?"
She says, "My child has gone to trade."
"What took he when he went?"
"His spear and hafted-knife, Provider."
Among the houses where he went, of what sort does he sleep in?
She said, "I only know he went. He sleeps where men have built, Lujoge."
He said, "I also have a spear and hafted knife.
I sleep where men have built.
Don't worry about your yams–they're not the food I want to eat.
I am a trader. I have enshankala plantains."
The elder woman returned with me.
At home she offered lodging. I agreed.
When evil roams,
When a person faints and does not revive . . .
As I was there,
The sun was in its evening hour,
I met their neighbor.
We spoke of many things.
He said, "Let's make the cut, sir. Let's swear blood brotherhood this day!"
He said, "And when night gives way to the early light of dawn,
And you resume your trading trip,
Let me guide you through the wilderness.
There,
In the land of wild animals,
Let me guide you to Kakili,
One Kakili village then the other."
When night gave way to the early light of dawn,
My blood brother came promptly.
He guided me to Kakili.
We passed the other Kakili too.
The sun emerged from the water.
And when the sun had emerged from the water,
He said, "My blood brother, now you can't get lost."
After I had passed the one Kakili then the other,
I heard a cracking sound before me in the brush.
I looked. There was no buffalo in there,
Great curved horn, the Hanging-noose.
There was no lion, the Roan-beast.
No elephant, the Elder of the wilderness.
When I looked I found a rhino, Shrill-cry,
Its eye I likened to a small hole in a dark ceiling.
Ya-a-a
I dropped the hafted knife and spear.
I kept the bow and quiver.
Ya-a-a
May one fly from me, find its way, and bite.
I let an arrow fly.
It found its way. It caught him.
He approached and spied me.
I hid behind a termite mound
And found that mound just right for me.
A hole opened and wrapped me in its cover.
He obliged: he turned away, Shrill-cry,
Calves with heavy foot-falls,
His eye, a small hole in a dark ceiling,
Come, you are aided by ngege.
Yo-o-o
He knelt before me.
As I studied him,
I thought, "He's studying me, the rhino."
But then I see his belly slacken.
I toss a clod of earth on him.
I see he does not stir.
I arise from the hole.
I cut the tail.
And then I mount his belly.
I whoop and ululate.
"You men of Ihangilo,
I've killed the Calf-that-does-not-hinder.
I've killed Lwangege,
His eye, a small hole in a dark ceiling."
I heard the men of Ihangilo with their hunting dogs and iron bells.
They blew their horns.
They said, "Leave off!
A lousy Kymutwaalan come and kill the grey-one?
The grey-one's beaten a native son–
Was it killed by a lousy Kyamtwaalan?"
I say, "Don't be ashamed.
Go ahead and cut it open.
You'll find my arrow in there."
They agreed. They cut it open.
They took out an engona arrow.
They said, "Don't be ashamed, you men of Nyalubamba,
Missile that strikes the place of danger,
He that enters 'Warrior's-Death' with laughter on his lips,
Fire-forbidden, Iron-one,
From the home of Missile-strikes-a-bull's-back,
Keeps watch 'til dawn, Dried leaves, Iron hammer,
Tail-cutter and Non-knower."
I heard them say, "Don't be ashamed, child.
Let the man of Kyamutwaala take the rhino."
They let me take the front horn and the back.
I put them with the tail I'd cut.
I'd slept one night away from home.
What happiness that day, my kings!
I did not sleep away again.
I go and stand on a hill,
As I might stand at Lyamaholo Bugengele.
As I travel along, I whoop and ululate.
I go and yell it loud.
I add the sound of my buffalo horn,
I'm just like a trader.
My wife heard the buffalo horn.
She met me in the forecourt of the house.
–You know how women always peek inside.–
She came laughing the laughter of weaverbirds,
Her molars showing on both sides.
She welcomed me, coming from the hearth.
Then she went and peeked inside.
She found the rhino horns I'd wrapped in bast.
The tail was also there.
She searched, but nothing was there to eat: no piece of meat, no fish.
She said, "You, husband!"
She'd been laughing in the forecourt, the whole way from the hearth.
But approaching then, she did not laugh.
"What is it, mother?
What is it, Honored one?
What is it, Heart of our people,
Arms that roll me,
Hands caress me:
Things you give to me."
He said, "What did I do?"
"My husband,
You don't just make me laugh–you KILL me.
Cattle horns you left in Kianja!
Did you trade for them in Buzinja?"
I felt anger seize me.
Then she pressed banana mash,
But I refused it.
I said, "I'm tired. I've come from trading."
Of the plantains I ate half a piece.
When night had almost given way to the early light of dawn,
I took my things and went to trade.
The low road I descended to Mafumbo.
I looked toward Bujumba.
The lake's not wide there.
I crossed the water.
I travelled to Bumbile Island.
I travelled to trade the goods of Buzinja.
Let's go.
I descend toward the west.
I descended to Makongola by the low road at Kijunjula's.
I took the road past Lwailundu.
Kitinde stream sparkles and bobs.
I took the pass between the mountains.
In Kazila-mfuka at Lutizibwa's in the forecourt
I took the road to Luhija.
I came to Kamilondo mountain.
All you of Nyakijuganwa
Aha-a-a
Ye-e-e
Ye-e-e
Ye-ye-ye Ye-ye-ye
I'll drink it at Kafunjo or at Kitala of Mpa.
Wewe
Straight, straight, straight ahead, I shuffled.
There in Nyakibimbili I drank it at Manueli's.
You men of Katome,
Of Kagabilo in Bajuga,
Ha-a-a
In Mbale at Kyabuganzi's
And Lutiluuti's in the forecourt
I knew the road to Kagabilo
I took the one to Luhunga and Kashaalu.
At the wide ferry I ferried across at Kansinda.
From Kaigembi I travelled to Ishozi of Nshalaazi.
I took the road to Nkenge.
I saw the road to Bugolola.
Weye-e
Ho-o-o
She went to sleep after abusing me.
I bring these matters, The Spotted-one.
At Kyaka,
I'd not yet reached the ferry on the Kagera when
I reached Lunywebwa.
I went to Kenkondo.
I went to Malenge.
When I reached Kituku in the forecourt,
I asked for Kyembunda of Muguta,
Muguta of Kabundama, the founder.
When I came to Kisimani,
I stopped at Kabumbilo's in Kisimani.
I hailed across the river in Kitala of Menge,
"You men of Kitala of Menge,
Paddle me across. I'll pay"
They agreed.
Mpeta Malobo took me in his canoe.
I crossed to the other shore.
When I reached the palace of Nyamutula,
Columns in Kakoko's house have no storage,
"Please show me the road for Ankole."
They say, "Go west."
I shuffled along.
When I came to Ankole,
I found men pasturing cattle.
These were the first I met.
"You, man, what seek you in trade?"
I say, "I seek bulls and cows."
"What have you brought?"
I say, "I have the tail of a rhino, Shrill-cry.
I have both the rhino's horns."
"Enough. We'll take you to our king.
He's in the kraal."
When they had brought me to the kraal, I showed the rhino's things.
The front horn he traded for sixty cattle.
And the back one brought fifty.
The tail I traded for a herd of goats.
"Let me return now, Good One."
I turned toward home and started out with my cattle.
I crossed over.
When I approached our village–
There was a hill: as at Bugengele you can hear all the way to Lyamaholo–
I blew my horn just like a trader.
Whoever stands there asks,
"Has he come, Lujoge?
Has he come, the Revealer of glad tidings?
Has he come, Shows-cheer-in-adversity like the mouth?"
It travels like a creeper vine.
May you lack the space to seat your guests.
The packet by a lover wrapped–
Its coffee berries you eat;
It falls on its back
A leather kilt works
Floating plants of the Kagera River
Waves of Lake Victoria of the Great-calfed one.
My friend,
He-who-knows-himself eats not the food of children in a small basket.
Ha-a-a
Ha-a-a
I had great strength.
I saw my wife come running.
I met her in the forecourt.
When she saw the cattle on this side and that,
And goats pouring all over our farm,
She did not look at me at all.
That day she jumped and ran to the hearth.
She tore off ripe bananas.
She squeezed sweet mash for me.
Yo-o-o
I did not drink it.
You don't love me.
You love the things.
That day she squeezed sweet mash.
I refused it, Lujoge.
I said, "I still feel tired, mother.
I've come from trading."
She cooked some food.
I broke off half a plantain.
I said, "I feel tired.
I've come from trading."
Soon it was evening just as now.
She went out on the pathway to the farm.
She brought back herbs: Sweetness-of-lushakala,
Kashunshu-leaves-that-fall-themselves,
Nurses-get-ready,
Soul-flees-them,
Heart-stealer.
She did herself for me with Stray's-Return.
May-nettle-sting-all-over.
Return-to-where-it-stubs-the-toe,
Eshube kishanja.
They sigh covetously at what they cannot carry
On Nyabeteka mountain in Luhanga.
I heard two clans fill the entrance.
I said, "What is it, my wife?"
my child, I knew she was powdering herself at the hearth.
Her private vessel she took from beside the bed–
They call it by the name of Off-they-race.
When she lay down
She found me escorted off by snoring.
"Father, king who rules all peoples, elder,
Turn to me, dear one, my bloodbrother.
Aren't you Drinks-not-as-a-child?
My husband, Drinker-of-fermented-brew, say who gave sweet mash to you, and you agreed?"
That day she continued,
"Lover-of-the-folding,
Lover-of-the-draw-in-wrestling,
Come let's fold each other–
Young ones fold,
The elders draw in wrestling."
I said, "Don't bother me.
I'm tired.
I've come from trading."
Yai ya-a-a
We lay down, we slept.
When night had risen,
She went to cultivate.
When she had gone to cultivate
–Was there in the planted fields–
I fainted dead away.
I fainted as in sickness.
The child I had with her
Went running
And found her in the planted fields.
He said, "Mother! Mother! Mother! You!"
He said, "Be stopped from cultivating! May Nyakalembe strike you down!"
He said, "May she strike you down. May she strike you down completely."
He said, "And may Ilimamabuye
Bury you in a tilled field."
On Bumbile Island they call her Kikuli.
They call her Promise-keeper.
They her call Ashes-of-Burnt-Grass.
In Ihangilo they call her Baboon-killer.
In Kihanja she is Shaker-out-of-the-sheep's-Buttocks,
Water-furrow
Of-the-Buffalo
Swampy-sand.
In Karagwe she is Father of Wounds.
Ah-ya-a-a
In Kiziba, the home of measured speech,
They call her Nyabuleza Food-bringer.
At our home, among us Bankango
We call her by the name of Whiskers in which paper wasps are hidden.
Pruning hook that chops among the branches,
Relentless one of the beehive,
Great arms that subdue all.
"May she hit you with her leather kilt.
If you do not die, become an invalid.
Embracing, may you be met by a backside.
In Kyamutwaala may all know you well!
May you see the Stealer of things not hers,
Who comes not in diviner's dreams."
Weye-e-e
"The Ruler of all peoples, the Straightener.
–Come and let me show you, mother–
The man who drinks fermented brew,
I saw them giving him sweet banana mash.
His mouth dripped bile.
His nose dripped mucus.
His solar plexes . . .[were cold]
Stop for tape change
Ya-a-a
Be stopped from cultivating! Be slain by her!"
He said, "I saw them give him milk. He refused.
But sweet mash - no! no! - that's what he drank
My kings!
His mouth dripped bile.
His nose became cold."
"May lamentations be raised for you, my child."
He said, "He does not speak."
He said, "I saw that all the clans arrived.
We were so many that the banana shoots were trampled."
I snapped one finger on another,
I cracked one finger on another,
Until my joints began to swell.
That day, Provider,
Ha-a-a
Aya-ya
"Mother do not kill me.
Consult Bachwezi spirits."
I came with my hoe pointed at the Sun,
Great branches of Luhuga,
Child of others,
Wakens at dawn,
Not long in foot,
He favors raised, imploring palms,
Child of others,
Among elders he speaks.
When I came up our walkway,
I found the place filled with people.
When I met my husband's sister,
She said, "Mother, the Lover-of-the-folding, the Lover-of-the-draw-in-wrestling
She said, "See, your husband, the Folder:
Now they have separated."
She said, "They are parted."
My hoe I planted, still directed at the Sun.
Investigating, I found my husband laid out near the hearth.
I touched him on the mouth.
I find it drips bile.
When I touched him on the solar plexis,
He was cold, cold, cold.
Yo-o-o
His anus dripping ooze.
What shall I do?
I did not wail in grief
Lest I sever my husband's soul.
I moved self-consciously, as women do in raffia skirts.
I took hold of my resolve.
I clambered on the bed.
I got his spear.
You know we women save together with our men:
We had a thousand cowries in the roof peak.
I poked them down and did not bother counting.
When I went outside with them,
And I walked around the farm,
I cut all kinds of twigs:
The mutongo vine is first for divining.
Small-fruit mutula; I cut from the mulinzi shrub.
A mutunzo.
I hurried and cut mushambya.
All kinds of twigs I bundled into bundles.
I'll go make divination. He will not die.
My man has never eaten from another, Lujoge.
Bwogi's place is first,
In Kimbugu of Businga:
Cut goat's hooves in the morning,
In the evening they go to cultivate.
In Buzinja it's said with broken words.
In Kyamutwaala it's said in lamentation.
She's lost a husband with a big penis.
The women did not eat.
I heard vaginas in the village gathered in lament.
'She's been bereaved of the Testicle-Shaker.'
I saw they did not eat.
Hiyo-o-o
Near the hearth, the women lay with empty cooking pots.
He did the water oracle for me.
He said, "He will not die. He's eaten nothing."
I scorned the man's words.
I snatched from him the consultation fee.
I'll go to Kimuli
Hot Head . . .
Broken-plant-shoot of Kalemela
Why-do they-bother-me and They-break-me
Obscene-speech-of-the-Baziba
Long stick of the crow
Bird of the Apportioner of Elephants
He cuts the Bull on its back.
Hayah
He-e-e he-e-e
A Spotted-one, a king,
Elder bull
Ha-a-a
Bead-cutter you are.
Ha-a-a a Bull
Appear, Of-the-Bracelet
Sweet smell of rotting wood, in rainy season you make sheep cough.
He did the water oracle for me.
He didn't settle anything.
I'll go to Kasana's.
In Ihangilo those who do not know him
Call him Kasana, pouring himself along.
But we who know him
Call him Katete-grass, whistling in the wind.
He did the water oracle for me.
He didn't settle anything.
I run and arrive at Ilimamabuye's.
On Bumbile Island she is Kikuli.
They call her Promise-keeper.
They call her Ashes-of-Burnt Grass.
In Ihangilo she is Baboon-Killer.
In Kihanja, Shaker-out-of-sheep's-buttocks,
Water-furrow,
Swampy-sand.
In Karagwe she is Father of Wounds.
And in Kabale we name her Whiskers
In which paper wasps are hidden,
Pruning hook that chops among the branches,
Relentless one of the beehive,
Great arms that subdue all.
He did the water oracle for me.
He did the water oracle for me.
On that day.
What shall I do?
My man . . .
I cried aid from the Sun,
Great branches of Luhuga
Child of others
I heard the oracle refuse.
I consulted Bachwezi. I used them all.
Now what shall I do?
When I was returning,
On a cattle path one shouldn't reveal one's woes.
I said, "My kings . . .
I cannot bewail what has been stolen.
I am bereaved of a husband."
Ho-o-o
Rather than eat of love,
Eat something bitter from a spirit;
You promise and in a growing-season settle the debt.
But wondrous love's debt never has been settled by a human.
An old woman stood among the plantain trees.
She said, "What's wrong, my child?
For you speak aloud what's in your heart.
Can you not bewail what has been stolen?
Are you bereaved of a husband or a lover?
I see your raffia skirt has left its mark.
Your hair stands on end.
My lady, dust clings to you.
Your belly's gone for four and four, eight days–today, the ninth–
It seems you have nothing inside."
Shi-i-i
She said, "I've lost a husband."
"Do not mourn. But go.
Your husband will not die.
He has not eaten from another.
Go down to Mishenye at Lwemihanda's.
There dwells a man who sees the future.
He'll tell the truth to you."
Then the low road I took down to Kigabilo.
I went toward Kitobo.
Ho-o-o
Kabula-ishoke.
At Kyanyabakaikulu I descended.
I came by the road to lower Kigabilo.
I crossed over.
At Nziba,
Lugasha I passed through.
When I took the road to Kajumilo,
Straight for the big open fields there,
I hear people joyous–
Like herders who have brought their cattle to the gate.
They sang, "If it's dead, toss it.
The rotten one has none to bury it."
I said, "What shall I do?"
I hear women's song:
"The white mushroom is happy. You are happy in your reign.
If the King precedes us
In the wilderness, what is there for us to fear?"
Look now!
But when I looked into the open fields
I saw no people there.
I saw only shanje grass and wilderness.
That man has been the death of me!
I've fallen in a place that's filled with spirits!
This day, today, I meet Nyakalembe face to face!
What I tell you did happen, Ta Dionies!
What I tell you did appear that day.
What I tell you did appear.
I saw Nyakalembe in the lead.
Ilimamabuye followed her.
When they came before me,
"You woman dressed in raffia skirt
Whose cinch has left its mark.
You're dusty.
You have the look of one who never swallows.
Your hair stands on end.
Can you not bewail what has been stolen?
Are you bereaved of a husand
Or are you bereaved of your lover?
Dear one, Mother-cow's-call
Dear one, Shows-cheer-in-adversity.
The deeds love does
Are those the spotted serpent speaks in the heart."
Soothing-warmth of an antelope pelt,
Bubbling-heat of a civet cat, .
Mosquitoes prick me like thorns, .
Breasts prick me like iron points. .
It falls on its back.
A leather kilt works.
Alight on me, you are a dainty one.
Come within, you are Kaihyula.
Wewe-e-e
"I've lost Knows-himself.
I've lost Eats-not-the-food-of-children."
A Spotted-one died in Bugabo.
He brought iron from Itahwa.
The bickering of women
That quickly overturned their reign.
They said, "Because you've told us everything–
You've lost your husband, not your lover–
You sit on the road's left side.
We'll sit on the road to the right.
You, wife in search of her husband,
In search of his soul where you were instructed,
Now don't go to another spirit.
Go to Wamara
Essential-Killer
Bows-and-Bends
Supple-one of Ndahula.
His son is Kagolo
Babbler/wobbler
Adorned with beads all over
Throws-himself-into-battle-with-warriors.
They cut it in Nongo wood,
It fell in Kabushekya.
Honey in the rock
Is not drawn out easily with fire.
He killed a man in Kihanja,
He boasted of it at his uncle's in Kiziba of the rock.
Now, now,
We'll tell the truth.
Go to Wamara
Essential-Killer
Bows-and-bends
Supple-one of Ndahula.
Do not fear what you will find there.
At the entrance road, you'll find Hare the Avenger..
When you reach the palace, you'll find Crow, Big-Man with Claws, guarding the door.
Great palace entrances are not of great concern
Small forecourts in the palace are of great concern.
Pass by Wamara's to the upper section of the farm,
Where brewing spirits decant beer, the Balanzi.
As you pass, fear not, do not be shaken.
You'll find them decanting beer, the Balanzi.
Ha-a-a
For a fermentation vat, they use a grave.
Frog steps one foot into the tub.
Chameleon draws off mash.
Human shin-bone is the funnel.
Human blood is the mash.
They've woven together a dead man's ribs–that's the sieve, My kings!–
The brewing sieve for squeezing out the mash.
Hair is medium for the mix.
Human heads, the beer seats.
Snakes are the working ropes tied to tubs.
A man laid out, the stone they grind the sorghum on.
But the sorghum that they grind–
They're grinding human eyes.
A human palm, the upper millstone.
Do not fear. Enter. Tread lightly.
Go there and enter in.
You'll find him seated on a copper stool.
Remember: Do not fear.
If events confound you, sit down: fold your raffia skirt in the palace.
Woi
Fold your raffia skirt.
Wewe-e-e
Go, fold your raffia skirt and kneel.
Go, say your salutations like a man.
Say, 'Shumaalamu, Provider, Shumaalamu.'
Go, hear the way he answers you, Essential-Killer Wamara.
Hear the way he answers you."
I shuffled along and reached the Crow.
"Father Big-man with Claws
I know you are a Muziba.
I go to salute King Wamara."
He said, "I grant you my permission. Don't let your feet delay."
When I had come, I met Hare the Avenger.
He said, "I grant you my permission. Don't let your feet delay."
Therein is "Escort him here!"
Therein is "Return. We've got him!"
Therein is "Tell him to have him beaten!"
Yai!
Ye ye ye
Where an infant totes the baggage.
Yo-o-o
Therein is "Tell him to have him beaten!"
Therein at the home of Wamara, Essential-Killer
At Reedy-swamps Heave-up-and-down
At "Escort him here!"
Those who died without disease,
Those barred from paying consultation fees.
At the throne of Wraps-it-up– Listen, my friend,
"Don't be afraid in there,
Don't be afraid in there at all."
I went running.
And when I arrived at the forecourt gate,
I looked in at the royal house.
My eyes said, "Look to the upper section of the farm."
I found Balanzi spirits decanting beer.
Others were toting ripe bananas.
Those stripping them from a stalk, my friend, were putting them in a grave.
As I watched, Frog washed his legs.
He stepped one leg into the beer tub.
I saw Chameleon:
When it did not flow
He got into the mash.
I saw the mash was human blood,
I go I see shin bones, the funnels,
Ribs woven together, the sieve.
I saw him press the mash into the brewing vessel of the grave.
Beer seats I also saw there, my kings!
Seats in the vessel–
Human heads.
A man lying on his back–
They grind sorghum on his belly.
Eyes, the sorghum.
A human palm, the upper millstone.
Weyei yei
Weyei
Just as they told me, I did not fear.
I came to the palace.
I folded my raffia skirt.
I started greeting like a man.
I stopped greeting like a woman.
"Shumaalamu, Father of men, Wamara"
He swore, Reedy-marsh Heaves-up-and-down,
He truly swore.
He swore, the Enanga,
He said, "Since I've lived in the spirit world,
I have never been saluted by a living being
Nor have I been saluted by a woman dressed in raffia.
Where are you from, woman?
You, who come to Where-no-one-goes, to Where-iron-needles-are-worn.
Begin, report. What's been stolen?
Can you not bewail what's been stolen?
Are you bereaved of a lover?
Or are you bereaved of a husband?"
I said, "Father Provider,
You understand as though already told."
"Ascend to my royal house.
Go and bring me coffee berries.
I will return your husband's soul to you."
That day, Father-Creator.
When I ascended to his royal house,
They handed me the coffee berries.
The saucer they handed me was a human ear.
The coffee berries were human eyes.
They placed a napkin in my hand:
My lord, it was a human palm!
I did not refuse. I swallowed hard. I brought the things.
When I had brought these things before him,
He said, "You, woman, wembaga kinehe?
Where are you from?"
I said, "Where-iron-needles-are-worn.
I come from Buyonya,
I've come to search.
Spirits travel here,
They have followed me."
He said, "Your husband will not die.
He's eaten nothing of another.
Let me show the way to you, woman.
I'll wash away your journey's dust.
I'll make you stout, for today you've lost your stoutness.
Go to the shoreline of the Bull's Lake.
Go get what the lake has vomited up–
So your husband will be vomited up by the Balanzi spirits.
Go cut Falls-and-rises–
That is, Sharpened-points,
That is, raffia palm–
So your husband will arise from the Balanzi spirits.
Go cut papyrus–
The one they call Lanky boy child–
May your husband . . .
Go, find the water-lily Never-swallowed,
So your husband will revive and leave the Balanzi.
Cut him a staff of conqueror wood (musinga).
So your husband will conquer (singa) the spirits.
Your seed-bull I return to you."
He said, "When he grows up, you can plant his seeds."
While I was there, where he spoke to me,
At home, the ones with Kajango heard . . .
He sneezed!
He begin to fart!
His sisters there began to sang,
"'Uncover, let me look.
Uncover, let me look.
Uncover, let me look.
Uncover, let me look.
All good the things are not yet in view.'"
I took the road
I was brought by the road.
I left there in a hurry . . .
Stop for tape change
[Singer: Find me a little something to drink. --
Host: Go on, Sir, go on]
I came to the lake by that road.
I took from the lake its vomit.
I cut a staff of conqueror wood.
I ran and searched for Falls-and-rises.
I searched for water-lily, Never-swallowed.
I brought the water-lily, Never-swallowed
A staff of conqueror wood...
When I made sure I had them all,
My lord, Provider,
I met him then in the forecourt.
I came with a staff of conqueror wood
And water-lily, Never-swallowed,
Falls-and-rises, Sharpened-points,
And lake sand, which is the lake's vomit.
He met me there.
The raffia skirt had left its mark.
A wife was forgotton,
And a husband, almost forgotten.
[Then she began to ask me, "Child, my kings–"]
My lord, eyes never forget someone you've seen.
When I saw her I said, "My wife, my friend,
Why pass me by?
Don't I look like the man who married you?"
She said, "You do look like the one who married me, my husband."
"Where were you?"
She said, "When I found out you died,
I went to Wamara's to find your soul."
"You speak wrongly, my wife.
When I left Buzinja of Nshanshame, we were together.
I brought back horns of the rhino, Shrill-cry.
I added in the tail, but you did not relent.
You'd abused me.
You'd abused me.
You'd begun, 'Child, you bring no wealth.'
You'd gone to fetch water.
You used the traders for my abuse.
You said, 'They'd carried so many things they were throwing some away.'
I said, 'If they were throwing some away, my wife,
Why didn't you bring me just a few so I might see them too?'
You said, 'You're worth less than the beer jug at our house, Half-full.'
You said, 'You're worth less than the dove, the Trilling-voice.' My kings!
I said nothing . . .
I said, 'Set beer bananas to ferment.
Leave off! I'll travel in trade.
I'll go. I'll bring the things, my wife.'
I spent one day there.
On the second I returned.
A rhinoceros I killed, the Shrill-cry, the Foot-fall,
His eye, a small hole in a dark ceiling.
When you came to welcome me,
You welcomed me with open arms.
Into the forecourt you came, showing molars on both sides.
The laughter you were laughing was like that of weaver-birds.
But when you left the hearth,
You showed me only laughter in the nose.
'Cattle horns?' you said, 'They're in Kihanja.
What now, my husband? Do you bring them from Buzinja?'
Anger gripped me, and your plantains I did not eat.
I went to Ankole Katoke, and I traded for heavy bullocks.
I added milking cows.
I brought red bulls.
'A bull I'll choose,' I thought.
'I'll butcher it for her,
And she'll take from it a tasty bit of meat.'
I brought goats.
I added sheepskins.
Now, that's how I brought the wealth, child,
That's how you don't love me. You only crave my wealth, my kings.
That's how I fainted and lost consciousness.
Tell me of the White and the Big-spot,
The ones from which you got a tasty bit of meat,
Or of the White and the Big-spot,
The ones from which you drank rich milk.
Now, my kings,
I forgive you.
I forgive you, my wife.
Return inside.
Women are betrayers.
They make trouble in ways you cannot (foresee)
